quotedTweet.rawContent:
you’ll get mad at me for saying this…but cloud gaming is so obviously more economically efficient than physical hardware I think it’s going to be the default soon.

your home console / pc is idle 90%+ of the day. meanwhile, data centers targets what, 5%, maybe at worst 10% idle.
 
every second a cloud gamer isn’t gaming, that hardware is being used for someone else, training, etc.
 
I think there should be a new measurement, something like cost-per effective FLOP hour that takes into account the TCO + effective utilization. 

If a gamer spends $500 on a GPU, uses it for 3 years, but it’s only fully active ~5% of that period…the cost-per relative FLOP hour is crazy high! Meanwhile, a $50,000 datacenter GPU might have a *LOWER* cost-per FLOP hour just because the effective utilization is 90+%.

tweets.content:
RT @jukan05: Potential attractive investment opportunities for 2H26 —

I still think they are likely to be Chinese semiconductor localization names.

A few days ago, I had meetings with Chinese sell-side analysts, and they said CXMT’s market cap immediately after IPO could be at least RMB 5 trillion.

It seems that many Western investors are underestimating the investment frenzy around CXMT within China. I was also surprised when I first heard the RMB 5 trillion number, but I don’t think it is completely unreasonable.

Most of the capital raised through CXMT’s IPO will likely flow into Chinese semiconductor localization stocks.

Names like ACMR and NAURA look promising.

I also think shorting the Apple supply chain could be an interesting idea… but there are not many Apple supply chain names with low AI exposure.

Maybe CRUS is one of the few?

But isn’t that itself a bearish point on Apple? If the Apple business were truly strong and attractive, why are so many Apple vendors trying to move away from Apple exposure and gain AI exposure instead?

tweets.content: RT @Gaurab: Samsung, SK Hynix, and TSMC source 80% of their WF6 from Japan. Two Japanese companies produce 25% of the world's tungsten hexafluoride. Both Kanto Denka and Central Glass are stopping all WF6 production on July 1. We manufacture chemicals. WF6 is a chemical. The semiconductor industry never asked what it's made from. WF6 deposits the tungsten contact plugs inside every advanced chip on Earth. There is no substitute for running production lines. Qualifying a new supplier takes 12 to 18 months. Building new capacity takes two to three years. China controls 80% of the tungsten powder WF6 is made from. Japan's tungsten imports from China have been zero since February. The two producers survived on stockpiles for five months. The stockpiles are gone. WF6 prices are up 232.7% year over year. Upstream tungsten powder is up 557%. Only six companies produce 90% of the world's supply. Two just quit. Every AI accelerator, smartphone chip, and SSD depends on a gas about to lose a quarter of its supply.

tweets.content:
RT @Unveiled_ChinaX: China’s aggressive campaign to dethrone the U.S. dollar has officially hit a wall. It turns out that authoritarian power has its limits: "Beijing can create financial instruments, but it cannot decree demand."

Despite over a decade of state-directed economic muscle, global markets are quietly rejecting the renminbi (RMB). The hard numbers reveal the true scope of this failure:

- The RMB still accounts for less than 5% of global trade transactions.
- A staggering 80% of transactions on China’s alternative network (CIPS) are still forced to route directly through the Western SWIFT system.
- Offshore yuan deposits sit at a minuscule $234 billion, completely buried by the $15 trillion in dollar-denominated assets held outside America.

True global reserve status requires deep markets, transparency, and the rule of law. Because the CCP refuses to loosen its authoritarian grip and strict capital controls, international businesses simply do not trust the currency enough to hold it voluntarily. Instead, real organic demand in emerging markets is flowing directly into innovations like dollar-pegged stablecoins, which exploded into a $317 billion market in just six years.

The CCP can lay down the financial tracks, but they cannot force the world to ride them.

Will China ever build the international trust needed to rival the dollar without completely giving up its centralized control? 

#DeDollarization #GlobalFinance #ChinaEconomy #CCP #Macroeconomics #Geopolitics

https://t.co/aQ506IR57E

RT @mullvadnet: The UK government spyware demand means that the government decides exactly what should be censored on every mobile device. They say they will start with nude pictures (if you don’t identify yourself as an adult). But it could at any time be expanded to anything the government disapproves of. Today, 30 people are arrested every day in the United Kingdom for writing something online that the government classifies as "grossly offensive". It is obvious that they will use this tool to restrict free speech.

Currently, there appears to be no requirement to report findings outside the device. However, with both legal and technological decision-making power taken away from individuals and transferred to the government, that is only a pen stroke away.

This means that the government could also use this system for total mass surveillance.

And they can do so in secret.

The government recently, in secret, tried to pressure Apple (which is now agreeing to client-side scanning) to build backdoors into its end-to-end encrypted cloud service. They can do this under the Investigatory Powers Act 2016, also known as the "Snoopers' Charter" – a law that makes it illegal for tech companies to disclose secret demands from the government.

tweets.content:
RT @askalphaxiv: Sakana Fugu Technical Report

Instead of training one larger model, Sakana AI trains an orchestrator that reads each query and dynamically routes or composes GPT-5.5, Gemini-3.1-Pro, Claude Opus 4.8 and other agents into query-specific workflows.

With Fugu being the fast router, and Fugu-Ultra being the deep multi-agent conductor, trained with SFT, evolutionary strategies and GRPO to build adaptive scaffolds.

The idea is to have the model pick GPT for math, Gemini for science and recall, Opus for debugging, then synthesize them when no single agent is best.

This router is able to get SoTA results across SWE-Bench Pro, Terminal Bench, LiveCodeBench, GPQA-Diamond, CharXiv and more, demonstrating the potential of orchestration being a practical alternative beyond training.

quotedTweet.rawContent:
you’ll get mad at me for saying this…but cloud gaming is so obviously more economically efficient than physical hardware I think it’s going to be the default soon.

your home console / pc is idle 90%+ of the day. meanwhile, data centers targets what, 5%, maybe at worst 10% idle.
 
every second a cloud gamer isn’t gaming, that hardware is being used for someone else, training, etc.
 
I think there should be a new measurement, something like cost-per effective FLOP hour that takes into account the TCO + effective utilization. 

If a gamer spends $500 on a GPU, uses it for 3 years, but it’s only fully active ~5% of that period…the cost-per relative FLOP hour is crazy high! Meanwhile, a $50,000 datacenter GPU might have a *LOWER* cost-per FLOP hour just because the effective utilization is 90+%.

RT @cr3ghost: How PatchGuard detects hypervisor-based hooks. Written by two Riot Games Anti-Cheat Engineers. Some of the most underrated researchers in the Windows internals and kernel/hypervisor security space.

Microsoft embedded multiple detection techniques inside PatchGuard to catch hypervisors hiding the real LSTAR MSR value. Trap flags, MOV SS tricks, ICEBP edge cases, LSTAR shadowing, and deliberately triggered page faults that expose the real syscall handler address.

Part 2 includes a custom detection technique with a full working PoC.

Part 1: https://t.co/pJNWLBDuQW

Part 2: https://t.co/nheDEfeDD1

PoC: https://t.co/e5PDrvaOj3

tweets.content:
RT @ptdbugs: Linux Kernel — net/skbuff: shared-frag marker not propagated through frag-transfer helpers (DirtyClone)

CVE: CVE-2026-43503
PT ID: PT-2026-42878
Vendor: Linux
Product: Linux Kernel (net/skbuff, XFRM/IPsec)
CVSS: 8.8
Credits: JFrog Security Research

Description:
A local privilege escalation issue exists in the Linux networking stack due to the improper propagation of the SKBFL SHARED FRAG flag during fragment transfers. Several helpers and functions, including  pskb copy fclone(), skb shift(), skb gro receive(), skb gro receive list(), tcp clone payload(), skb segment(), and skb try coalesce(), fail to carry over this marker when moving fragment descriptors. This creates a mismatch where a socket buffer (skb) may reference externally-owned or page-cache-backed pages while reporting that it does not have shared fragments via skb has shared frag().

This flaw can be exploited by in-place writers, such as ESP input (esp4.c, esp6.c), which rely on skb has shared frag() to determine if data must be processed through skb cow data(). An unprivileged user can leverage this to write into the page cache of a root-owned read-only file. Real-world exploitation, dubbed DirtyClone, allows an application to escalate privileges to administrator/root levels.

References:
• [https://t.co/NhWwYZwv4h

RT @bytecodevm: What the article doesn't say:
"Bypassing Defender" ≠ "bypassing EDR" — in a corporate environment, Defender is typically supplemented by Sentinel/CrowdStrike/SentinelOne, and the techniques described won't work there without additional layers (kernel-level telemetry, ETW patching, etc.)
Process injection into 7zFM.exe — detected behaviorally via the cross-process memory write pattern by any decent EDR.
Injecting into a legitimate process to mask network traffic — this is already beyond "signature bypass", this is active C2 evasion.

quotedTweet.rawContent:
Windows-Defenderの静的・動的検知を既知の3技法で回避するC++実装と詳細解説がGitHubで公開されています。ペイロードを暗号化してもローダー側のAPI一覧（IAT）で足がつく理由、ダイレクトシステムコールでIAT検査とAPIフック監視を同時に迂回できる仕組み、正規プロセスへの注入でネットワーク挙動の動的検知をすり抜ける方法。各段階のテスト結果付きで解説された2部構成のペンテスト教育記事です。

【パート1の要点】

・Windowsの実行フローの整理。アプリケーションの処理はKernel32[.]dll→Ntdll[.]dll→syscall→カーネルの順で到達する。Ntdll[.]dllがユーザーモードとカーネルモードの橋渡し役であり、パート2のダイレクトシステムコールが効く理論的前提

・AVとEDRの違いの整理。AVはシグネチャ照合やサンドボックスによる予防的検知、EDRはカーネルレベルの挙動監視で異常を検出。本記事はAV（Defender）回避に限定し、EDR回避は別課題

・シェルコードインジェクションの基本パターン「Allocate→Write→Execute」に沿い、VirtualAllocとmemcpyで展開・実行する基本C++ローダーを作成。Defenderの除外フォルダでは動作するが除外なしではシグネチャ照合で即検知。全回避策の出発点

・コンパイルフラグ（-s・-fno-exceptions・-fno-rtti・デッドコード除去等）でデバッグ情報や未使用コードを除去し、バイナリの情報量を削減

・テスト環境ではDefenderの「自動サンプル送信」の無効化が推奨されている。有効のまま開発すると検体がMicrosoftのクラウド分析に送信される可能性があり、開発中のバイナリがDefenderに検知されるリスク

・公開後にDefenderの検知が追いつくことを想定し、threatcheck[.]exeを使えば微修正で回避を維持できるとのヒント

【パート2の要点】

・シェルコードのXOR暗号化（鍵はコンパイルごとに変更）でディスク上のシグネチャ照合は回避。しかしローダー自体のIAT（Import Address Table＝プログラムが使うAPI関数の一覧表）にVirtualAlloc等が残り、ローダー側の一覧表も静的検知の対象となり得るため依然として検知

・SysWhispers2で通常のWindows API（VirtualAlloc等）ではなく下層のNT API（NtAllocateVirtualMemory・NtWriteVirtualMemory・NtProtectVirtualMemory・NtCreateThreadEx）を直接呼出し。Ntdll[.]dllの内部処理（syscall番号のセットとsyscall命令の発行）をアセンブリスタブで再現し、Ntdll[.]dll自体を経由せずカーネルへ直接到達する構成。Ntdll[.]dllを経由しないためIATに疑わしい関数名が残らず、Ntdll[.]dll上に仕掛けられたAVのフック監視も迂回。1手法で2検知層を迂回する構成。VS+MASMでのビルドが必要

・メモリ確保でPAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE（RWX＝読み書き＋実行の一括付与、セキュリティ製品の強い検知指標）を避け、PAGE_READWRITE→書込→NtProtectVirtualMemoryで実行権限を後付けする3段階方式に分離

・XOR復号の落とし穴。C++が鍵の文字列末尾に自動付加するヌルターミネーター（0x00）をキー長から除外（sizeof(key)-1）しないと、XOR 0x00＝元値のまま該当位置のバイトが復号されず、シェルコードが実行時に破損

・静的検知は突破できたが実行時に動的検知が発動。calc[.]exe起動に差し替えると検知なし→Defenderが検知しているのはインジェクション処理ではなくシェルコード側の活動と切り分け、リバースシェルのネットワーク接続を主因と見てネットワーク共有等での通信が想定される7zFM[.]exe（7-Zip）にNtOpenProcessで注入し、通信を正規プロセスの挙動に偽装して動的検知も回避

・同記事はこれらがAV（Defender）回避策であることを明示。EDR対策にはメモリ上の復号済みペイロードを非使用時に再暗号化する手法（Ekko等）が別途必要との注記

Defenderの各検知層に対する回避の実装と結果を確認できる構成で、防御側にとってもIAT検査やプロセス単位の挙動監視がどこで迂回されるかを具体的に確認できます。

Windows-Defenderの静的・動的検知を既知の3技法で回避するC++実装と詳細解説がGitHubで公開されています。ペイロードを暗号化してもローダー側のAPI一覧（IAT）で足がつく理由、ダイレクトシステムコールでIAT検査とAPIフック監視を同時に迂回できる仕組み、正規プロセスへの注入でネットワーク挙動の動的検知をすり抜ける方法。各段階のテスト結果付きで解説された2部構成のペンテスト教育記事です。

【パート1の要点】

・Windowsの実行フローの整理。アプリケーションの処理はKernel32[.]dll→Ntdll[.]dll→syscall→カーネルの順で到達する。Ntdll[.]dllがユーザーモードとカーネルモードの橋渡し役であり、パート2のダイレクトシステムコールが効く理論的前提

・AVとEDRの違いの整理。AVはシグネチャ照合やサンドボックスによる予防的検知、EDRはカーネルレベルの挙動監視で異常を検出。本記事はAV（Defender）回避に限定し、EDR回避は別課題

・シェルコードインジェクションの基本パターン「Allocate→Write→Execute」に沿い、VirtualAllocとmemcpyで展開・実行する基本C++ローダーを作成。Defenderの除外フォルダでは動作するが除外なしではシグネチャ照合で即検知。全回避策の出発点

・コンパイルフラグ（-s・-fno-exceptions・-fno-rtti・デッドコード除去等）でデバッグ情報や未使用コードを除去し、バイナリの情報量を削減

・テスト環境ではDefenderの「自動サンプル送信」の無効化が推奨されている。有効のまま開発すると検体がMicrosoftのクラウド分析に送信される可能性があり、開発中のバイナリがDefenderに検知されるリスク

・公開後にDefenderの検知が追いつくことを想定し、threatcheck[.]exeを使えば微修正で回避を維持できるとのヒント

【パート2の要点】

・シェルコードのXOR暗号化（鍵はコンパイルごとに変更）でディスク上のシグネチャ照合は回避。しかしローダー自体のIAT（Import Address Table＝プログラムが使うAPI関数の一覧表）にVirtualAlloc等が残り、ローダー側の一覧表も静的検知の対象となり得るため依然として検知

・SysWhispers2で通常のWindows API（VirtualAlloc等）ではなく下層のNT API（NtAllocateVirtualMemory・NtWriteVirtualMemory・NtProtectVirtualMemory・NtCreateThreadEx）を直接呼出し。Ntdll[.]dllの内部処理（syscall番号のセットとsyscall命令の発行）をアセンブリスタブで再現し、Ntdll[.]dll自体を経由せずカーネルへ直接到達する構成。Ntdll[.]dllを経由しないためIATに疑わしい関数名が残らず、Ntdll[.]dll上に仕掛けられたAVのフック監視も迂回。1手法で2検知層を迂回する構成。VS+MASMでのビルドが必要

・メモリ確保でPAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE（RWX＝読み書き＋実行の一括付与、セキュリティ製品の強い検知指標）を避け、PAGE_READWRITE→書込→NtProtectVirtualMemoryで実行権限を後付けする3段階方式に分離

・XOR復号の落とし穴。C++が鍵の文字列末尾に自動付加するヌルターミネーター（0x00）をキー長から除外（sizeof(key)-1）しないと、XOR 0x00＝元値のまま該当位置のバイトが復号されず、シェルコードが実行時に破損

・静的検知は突破できたが実行時に動的検知が発動。calc[.]exe起動に差し替えると検知なし→Defenderが検知しているのはインジェクション処理ではなくシェルコード側の活動と切り分け、リバースシェルのネットワーク接続を主因と見てネットワーク共有等での通信が想定される7zFM[.]exe（7-Zip）にNtOpenProcessで注入し、通信を正規プロセスの挙動に偽装して動的検知も回避

・同記事はこれらがAV（Defender）回避策であることを明示。EDR対策にはメモリ上の復号済みペイロードを非使用時に再暗号化する手法（Ekko等）が別途必要との注記

tweets.content:
RT @OSdev_: One of the most important Windows kernel data structures is "_EPROCESS".

Every running process has one. It is the kernel's representation of a process and contains everything Windows needs to manage it, PID, security token, handle table, virtual address space (VADs), thread list, PEB pointer, object table, and links to other kernel structures.

Security researchers love "_EPROCESS" because almost every kernel privilege escalation or rootkit technique interacts with it. Token stealing, process hiding (via "ActiveProcessLinks"), handle enumeration, memory forensics, and process injection all begin with understanding "_EPROCESS".

If you're learning Windows internals or kernel security, "_EPROCESS" is one of the first structures you should master.

RT @semivision_tw: The myth of “cheap” DDR5 from CXMT is just that—a myth. Its pricing is already comparable to that of Samsung, SK hynix, and Micron.

So even if Apple successfully adopts CXMT memory, the cost is likely to be similar to what it pays the three established suppliers. That raises the question: why bother?

The answer is leverage.

Apple doesn’t want to be held hostage by long-term agreements (LTAs), supply control arrangements (SCAs), or any single group of suppliers. By bringing in an alternative source, it gains greater bargaining power. Once the market stabilizes, it will be in a much stronger position to push back on pricing.

Looks like the challenger has finally entered the arena.

tweets.content:
LLMs can now autonomously generate fully functional Mythic C2 agents from a single prompt. Built, tested, and deployed in under 2 hours. No human in the loop.

SpecterOps built a framework called Oracle that takes a prompt, generates the agent code, compiles it, deploys it to a Mythic server, tests every command against a live Windows target, runs QA validation, and ships a release build.

Tested across Python, Go, Zig, C#, and Rust. Every generated agent is unique and disposable.

If every engagement gets a fresh LLM-generated agent, static signatures and YARA rules are fundamentally unable to keep up. Defenders need to rethink detection around behaviour, not artifacts.

https://t.co/pDxFso5wtk
